questions.specification: Which best defines task analysis in the UCD process?

options.content: Evaluating how users complete key actions

questions.specification:
Which element is most commonly represented in a user journey map?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXekFNHwRGv-JlJtH67c08o8mBS8k70x7D_OcVCY7tbhPmSpHFgTWcW6MIHryQ9DEkkNmlGVo3AKF9W7FG7jSZhfys2ejrn8yFJxwjj5IBEoel9jNew028PGjNDBT8vZq0rokqNnpQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
What is a direct result of effective persona creation?

A design company is creating a next-generation ergonomic backpack for students and commuters. Their design process incorporates feedback and observation to ensure comfort, usability, and user satisfaction.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A group of university students testing different backpack prototypes during a campus walk


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ca85287e-e0df-4323-91be-e40d57c1e398)

Identify one user-centred method evident in this figure.
parts.markscheme: - On-site user testing with real users <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two benefits of using this method for product development.
parts.markscheme:
- Reveals how users interact with the backpack in real-life settings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps detect comfort or balance issues early in design <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A designer conducting a user interview with a commuter about their current backpack’s strengths and weaknesses


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d6c0c6e1-9d1c-4b50-bfb7-40385e6732a8)

Explain why interviews are a valuable tool in a user-centred design approach.
parts.markscheme:
- Uncovers user preferences, frustrations, and expectations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps designers empathize with individual use cases <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables targeted improvements based on direct feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: Heatmap results showing pressure points on a user’s back while carrying a loaded backpack


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/445f98c6-3561-498c-b70d-2b0125eb5c14)

Explain two ways this type of data can inform the redesign of the backpack.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies areas where padding or weight redistribution is needed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages reshaping of the backpack to align with spine and shoulder ergonomics <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe three user-centred considerations that should be prioritised in this backpack design.
parts.markscheme:
- Comfort during extended wear <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ease of accessing compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Durability to withstand daily commuting or student use <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how a user-centred design approach could improve both user well-being and long-term product appeal for this backpack.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces physical strain, promoting better posture and comfort <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases user satisfaction by solving practical pain points <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages brand loyalty through positive experiences <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leads to thoughtful features (e.g. hidden compartments, adjustable straps) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes the product more inclusive across different body types <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aligns with user lifestyle, boosting marketability <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Which of the following would likely appear in the "Threats" quadrant of a product SWOT analysis for a UX design project?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a6f9e71c-9fb9-48be-945f-487b6e84ef2f.jpeg)

options.content: High competition in the app market
options.markscheme: Threats are external factors that can negatively impact a project or product. High competition is an external factor that the company cannot control but must mitigate, placing it in the Threats quadrant.
An intuitive layout is an internal positive attribute of the product, making it a Strength rather than a Threat.
An easy onboarding process is an internal positive attribute, which would be classified as a Strength.
High user retention is a positive internal metric, classifying it as a Strength.

questions.specification:
Why do designers use affinity diagrams in UCD?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfI5cG6V3rkZW7Mm6oD8io1czXLQjtJaNv7-U-_9NIvfffmIPTPd0RzH7-K0HZqCkJKxazWbpJb88UmeqNwvp-xv0IIZkp3usjfASjcgIjlbH95bJbOqpy7rm-4Lp6YRru6s2mKFQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: To group insights from user feedback

questions.specification: What role does empathy play in user-centred design?

What part of User-Centred Design (UCD) does this image support?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/77838069-0dc2-4916-b103-740c34e81291.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Market segmentation is a broader marketing strategy used to divide a target market into approachable groups, whereas this specific tool focuses on individual user empathy.
The diagram is a tool for human-centred qualitative research, not for defining AI automation logic.
The image shows an Empathy Map. This tool is used in UCD to gain a deeper understanding of the user by mapping out what they think, feel, see, hear, say, and do, as well as their pains and gains.
Manufacturing process evaluation focuses on the feasibility and efficiency of production, which is separate from the user research phase represented here.

questions.specification: A design consultancy has been hired to improve the experience of commuters using ticket machines at a busy train station. The team is applying user-centred design (UCD) principles to address user complaints and accessibility challenges.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A visually impaired user attempting to buy a train ticket using a touchscreen machine with no physical buttons


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/76567e86-7a75-4ca5-85a6-ba732555612e)

Identify one key usability issue presented in this scenario.
parts.markscheme: - Lack of tactile or audio feedback for users with visual impairments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the design team could apply user-centred design principles to address this issue.
parts.markscheme:
- Add voice-guided navigation to assist visually impaired users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include physical buttons or Braille indicators for tactile input. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A group of elderly users participating in a co-design workshop with the design team


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/542a4507-27a1-479f-9003-e98442882049)

Define co-design and explain its value in a user-centred design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Definition: Co-design is a process where end users actively participate in the design of the product. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Value: It ensures user needs are directly captured and reflected in the final design. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest two design considerations the team should prioritise when working with elderly users.
parts.markscheme:
- Use larger fonts and high-contrast colours for screen readability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simplify the interface to minimise the number of steps to complete a task. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A side-by-side comparison of two prototypes: one with a standard height, and one with a lowered and angled screen designed for wheelchair access


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c8b686f9-8cbc-43d9-9ce1-8d5fa3c40ca2)

Explain how physical ergonomics influence the design choice between these two kiosks.
parts.markscheme:
- An angled screen improves visibility and reach for seated users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A vertical screen may be difficult to use from lower angles or for people with limited upper body movement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the long-term benefits of applying user-centred design to public service products like ticket machines.
parts.markscheme:
- Increases user satisfaction by reducing confusion and error rates. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves accessibility, ensuring equal service for all users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances brand image and trust in public services. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces support costs by minimising need for staff intervention. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages wider adoption of self-service technologies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps identify and eliminate bias in technology design. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: In UCD, what is the purpose of targeting persona populations?

options.content: To empathise with and understand users’ demographics

questions.specification: Data gathered in user-centred research is mainly used to:
Personas are developed to:

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Packaging vendors are part of the supply chain, not user research.
Correct. Personas are fictional characters created to represent the different user types within a targeted demographic or user population.
Incorrect. This is a technical performance test, not a function of personas.
Incorrect. Machine tolerances relate to manufacturing precision, not human factors research.

parts.content: Describe how user-centred research can inform the creation of a user persona.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** and use research methods such as interviews, surveys, or observations to gather primary data from users <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Outline** the collection of demographic and psychographic information to provide a comprehensive profile of the target audience <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Analyse** the research data to identify common patterns in user behaviours, goals, and needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Construct** a fictional character (persona) that represents a typical user segment based on these identified patterns <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each step described in the persona creation process, up to a maximum of [4].</note>

questions.specification: Which activity would **not** typically fall under UCD research?

What does the image most clearly represent about user-centred design?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/493f7c44-df7a-45d8-90c4-89ade662b8e8.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Manufacturing tolerances refer to the permissible limit of variation in a physical dimension or property, which is a technical production consideration rather than a user-centred research method.

The diagram displays various user research strategies such as interviews, questionnaires, observations, and trials. These are structured methods used during the planning and research phases of user-centred design (UCD) to gather data about user needs and behaviours.
An algorithmic model is a mathematical or logical sequence used to solve problems or perform calculations, which is not what the user research methods in the diagram represent.
Tracking production output involves monitoring the quantity and quality of goods manufactured, which is a stage of the production process, not the user-centred design research phase.

questions.specification:
This image helps designers assess:

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdnwjEsQo4ZywTDXJ94WqTCTsqHMrXTgVOku4nopekZhzLnSFYsM6ycP3hOj07T5Xq-7qoLcZOtzi5OR7ydWxDM9keX7h6QlVd3LYhCD5Oc0cLv7wC6IgsNCNzeN6t93I_u02vc?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

A design consultancy is developing a public information kiosk for use in urban parks. The project team uses user-centred design methods to ensure accessibility, ease of use, and relevance to the local population.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A team observing how park visitors interact with a prototype touchscreen kiosk placed beside a pathway


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3d0a62b2-7f3f-431d-9a19-762cbf5a67ca)

Identify the user-centred research method being used in this situation.
parts.markscheme: - Observational research in a real-use context. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one benefit and one limitation of this method in the context of public kiosk design.
parts.markscheme:
- Benefit: Provides realistic insight into how users naturally approach and interact with the kiosk. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: May not capture user thoughts or frustrations unless follow-up questioning is conducted. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A designer testing different screen heights and button sizes with wheelchair users using a mockup



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f077b24c-a222-4c60-bb4f-a485d4f18d98)

Outline two ways the design team is applying inclusive design principles in this testing.
parts.markscheme:
- Adapting interface layout to suit varying user mobility and reach. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensuring button size and spacing accommodate users with reduced dexterity <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State three usability goals the kiosk design should aim to meet.
parts.markscheme:
- Easy navigation between different information screens. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clear readability in different lighting conditions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fast response time for touch interactions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A heatmap showing areas of frequent touch and usage on a digital interface after one week of user testing


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ca990ee9-505b-492f-ae7d-5eecf0c6fc78)

Suggest two possible design modifications the team could make based on this data.
parts.markscheme:
- Move most accessed functions to the centre of the screen for quicker access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduce clutter in areas with minimal interaction to improve clarity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how a user-centred design approach benefits both the end user and the city council funding the kiosk project.
parts.markscheme:
- Ensures the kiosk meets real user needs, improving satisfaction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances accessibility for all ages and abilities, increasing public engagement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces future maintenance or redesign costs due to early problem detection. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Builds community trust in public services through responsive design. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages continued use, providing valuable data and public information access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Demonstrates responsible use of public funds through inclusive, thoughtful solutions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
This template helps designers develop which of the following?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcGGVgygcCTTEv_Al0wHgC7zjeoAejsDDtO7W5wFmdoC-AK5_B47Mgpa6te4iCam1by093WWNTbUMfKkTqZP1gMZPTQxEUGlaxHqFkBMAR9DRyIQiRgRZixMnDlU7oCMlIfIZ7qVA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

A design consultancy is tasked with improving the safety and accessibility of a self-checkout system used in supermarkets. They use a user-centred approach to investigate diverse users’ experiences and gather design insights.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: Elderly customer struggling to use a self-checkout kiosk


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5b80cf32-a8fe-4e38-9460-5ef20a94bdda)

i.) Identify one potential usability issue evident in the figure.
parts.markscheme: - Small text or buttons may make the interface hard to read and use. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Suggest two user needs that should be prioritised when designing for elderly users.
parts.markscheme:
- Clear, large-text interfaces <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simplified instructions with minimal steps <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: Designer conducting contextual inquiry beside a user at a self-checkout



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7032d0e0-37d4-49f4-a29b-1111832061ac)

Explain the advantage of using contextual inquiry in this setting.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows observation of real-time user interactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Captures environmental challenges like lighting or noise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reveals genuine user behaviour and unspoken frustrations <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: Persona card of a “visually impaired user” showing tech usage and pain points


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cf2ffd69-8a05-4e89-a6d7-b627fc6e7512)

i.) Identify and explain two ways the interface can be improved to support Ava’s needs.
parts.markscheme:
- Add voice guidance to assist navigation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensure high-contrast text and button outlines <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation of how each feature supports her needs <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: ii.) State two reasons why inclusive design is important in public interfaces like self-checkouts.
parts.markscheme:
- It ensures accessibility for people with disabilities or diverse abilities, improving equity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases usability for a wider population, reducing frustration and improving adoption. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the stages of a user-centred design process the team should follow for this project.
parts.markscheme:
- Empathise: Conduct research to understand users’ pain points. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Define: Identify key usability challenges (e.g. visual layout, speed of interaction). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ideate: Generate solutions such as audio instructions or simplified flows. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prototype: Create basic versions of the interface for testing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Test: Have users of all ages and abilities interact with the prototypes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Iterate: Refine based on user feedback and retest. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Why are usability objectives analysed in user-centred design (UCD)?
UCD contributes to usability by:
What type of data should be prioritised in user-centred research?

parts.content: Discuss the importance of empathy in user-centred design.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explains** how empathy allows designers to understand users' feelings and pain points. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describes** how empathy fosters inclusive design for diverse populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identifies** how empathy helps designers anticipate user frustration or confusion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Discusses** how empathy leads to more effective solutions tailored to real needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explains** how empathy builds trust and emotional connection with users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outlines** how empathy encourages iterative improvement based on feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each well-explained point on empathy, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following is most aligned with task analysis in UCD?

What is the primary purpose of this usability testing lab setup?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/bd8ae66d-af6c-4e03-b56e-29bbd320e2d2.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Product life cycle assessment (LCA) is a technique used to assess environmental impacts associated with all stages of a product's life. It is not the purpose of a usability lab setup.
Quantitative market research usually involves large-scale data collection (e.g., surveys). A usability lab is typically used for qualitative observation of individual users.

options.content: Observing how users interact with a product
options.markscheme: A usability lab setup, featuring a one-way mirror and an observation room, is specifically designed to allow researchers to watch and record how a user interacts with a prototype or product without interfering with the user's natural behavior.

Building wireframes is a design activity used to create low-fidelity layouts of an interface. It occurs during the design phase, while the lab setup shown is for testing an existing prototype or product.

parts.content: State two types of research methods used in User-Centred Design (UCD).
parts.markscheme:
- User interviews <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Observational studies <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each correctly named method, up to [2 max]. Accept other valid research methods such as focus groups, questionnaires, or usability testing.</note>

questions.specification: What is the primary aim of user-centred design (UCD)?

options.content: To enhance a product’s usability by focusing on users’ needs throughout the design process

questions.specification:
What does the task flow chart primarily help designers understand in the user-centred design process?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXen4EmnQGjhJ56W05a5gsOKBravOuCPAqOA2GN4PGZIm0PsRxawhfNpZC90BPhy-roKKgJ7xX8F8bJ_HP_M-ExLorj97JagdJZCszu2MfR3B9aU3GVf9WYMOZtV2pFrM_fwq1e3?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: The sequence of steps a user takes to complete a task and where difficulties may occur
The visual layout and hierarchy of screens in a wireframe
The underlying data structure and relationships in a database schema
The aesthetic style guide for colours, typography, and branding

questions.specification: A task analysis can best help designers:

options.content: Understand how users interact with a product

questions.specification: What best supports empathy-building in User-Centred Design (UCD)?

options.markscheme: Empathy in UCD involves understanding the user's perspective, experiences, and difficulties. Surveying users about their challenges directly informs the designer about the user's needs and feelings, which is a core part of building empathy.
Benchmarking CPU speed is a technical performance measurement and does not involve understanding user emotions or experiences.
Testing tensile strength is a material property test and is not related to building empathy with the user.
Modelling fluid dynamics is an engineering simulation focused on physical behavior, not user empathy.

questions.specification:
Why is it important to interview a user like the one shown in the image?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfQc0Qw_GU7kk52bGfNV_GbLO6_KNeC2Iv_iZzJ3D1ReeyTp43iMYnp5AepSWgVxS-pozIGM45S7JawRyhBIrm7I-Va7uH6RPJariBcqZXGezHzC1PEOZzHKExL-si_Q_jQ-oNX?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: To reduce the cost of large-scale usability testing
To identify challenges faced by underrepresented user groups
To confirm the popularity of current design trends

questions.specification: Which is the most direct benefit of using persona research?

options.content: Clearer identification of target user segments
Better prioritisation of product features based on user goals

questions.specification: How can designers evaluate whether a product meets usability goals?

options.content: Compare it to top competitors’ sales

parts.content: Explain how persona development supports user-centred product design.
parts.markscheme:
- Personas represent archetypal users based on research. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They guide decisions on features and aesthetics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Help keep the team aligned on user needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourage empathy among team members. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitate testing of user journeys or scenarios. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allow prioritisation of features for primary users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each benefit explained, up to [6 max].*

[Total: 6 marks]

questions.specification: A designer running interviews and surveys is most likely trying to:

parts.content: Compare quantitative and qualitative research methods in the context of UCD.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that quantitative research methods focus on the collection of numerical data, for example through the use of surveys. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** that quantitative methods provide measurable and statistical insights into user trends and interactions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that qualitative research methods are used to explore user behaviour and emotions, for example through interviews.<marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** how qualitative methods yield an in-depth understanding of the motivations behind user actions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Contrast** the two by noting that quantitative results are generally easier to generalise to a wider population. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outline** how qualitative insights can uncover complex problems or user needs that numerical data alone cannot fully explain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each valid comparison, up to a maximum of [6 marks].</note>

questions.specification: Usability objectives in UCD help to:

options.content: Identify user needs for product improvement

parts.content: Define user-centred design (UCD) and explain its primary goal.
parts.markscheme:
- Define user-centred design (UCD) as a design approach that focuses on the needs, wants, and limitations of end users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the primary goal of UCD is to improve user satisfaction and usability by involving users throughout the design process <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for a correct definition and [1] for a clear explanation of its goal.</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following is **not** a feature of User-Centred Design (UCD)?

options.markscheme: Emphasis on user needs is a core principle of UCD, where the design is based upon an explicit understanding of users, tasks, and environments.
Prioritising manufacturing efficiency is a characteristic of Design for Manufacture (DfM). UCD focuses on the user's needs and usability over manufacturing constraints.
User-centred research (e.g., interviews and observations) is a fundamental method used in UCD to gather data about user requirements.
Developing personas to represent target user populations is a key tool used within the UCD process.

questions.specification: A company is designing a new electric toothbrush aimed at helping children aged 5–9 brush their teeth independently. The team conducts user-centred research and applies usability objectives to improve the design.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A product designer developed a children's electric toothbrush prototype to be tested by the target audience.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a8ec5137-c42e-4b5c-9608-75b15dd6507a)

Identify one user-centred research method being used in this scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- User observation <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] for a valid method of user-centred research identifiable from the scenario.

parts.content: State one advantage and one limitation of using this research method with young children.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Allows designers to observe natural behaviour and motor skill challenges. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: Young children may act differently when being observed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] for a plausible advantage and [1] for a plausible limitation related to user observation.

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A sample persona to show a child user’s traits, motivations, and brushing difficulties

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/68d8c2f7-7fe6-4b52-8047-436b48123a32)


Explain how using this persona could support empathetic design decisions.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps the team understand the child’s goals (e.g. independence). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reveals frustrations like awkward grip or difficulty reaching molars. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs child-friendly features like timer music or ergonomic handles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per relevant point explaining how persona development helps designers build empathy and tailor product features.

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A usability testing session where a child tries to use the toothbrush with minimal guidance


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8ee49551-06da-4420-9273-c6673598a2c1)

Identify two usability objectives relevant to evaluating the toothbrush and describe how each applies.
parts.markscheme:
- Learnability: Can the child understand how to use it without help? <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Satisfaction: Does the child enjoy the experience, making them want to brush regularly? <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] for each usability objective correctly identified and described in context.

parts.content: Evaluate how applying task analysis can help designers improve the product for this age group.
parts.markscheme:
- Breaks the brushing process into steps: turning on, brushing zones, rinsing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies where children struggle (e.g., button too stiff, grip too large). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs step-by-step instructional features or interactive feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps improve safety and comfort. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages empathy through seeing the process from the child’s point of view. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitations include variability in brushing habits across children. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per relevant, well-explained point on how task analysis aids design improvement for young users, up to [6 max].
